title: ZXBrick Complete Guide
description: A full text guide for ZXBrick covering gameplay flow, controls, levels, power-ups, progression systems, and practical improvement methods.
summary: This page holds the complete readable guide content for ZXBrick so search engines and accessibility users can access the full details in HTML text.

sections.heading: What ZXBrick is
paragraphs: ZXBrick is a retro-inspired brick-breaking game in the [ZX Series](/zx-series). It combines level progression, unlock systems, and replay-focused progression loops through achievements, leaderboards, and shared currency support.
Players typically progress through 100 handcrafted stages, build momentum through repeated sessions, and use achievement and leaderboard feedback to guide improvement over time.

sections.heading: Core gameplay loop
paragraphs: The core goal is to clear playable patterns, keep the ball active, and finish levels cleanly.
Players use short, repeatable sessions to build consistency before attempting longer progression attempts.
As sessions repeat, aim to improve one goal at a time: fewer mistakes, clearer level starts, or higher score quality in one level type.

sections.heading: Controls and input rhythm
paragraphs: Settle into a consistent input pace before accelerating gameplay tempo.
Small, deliberate inputs reduce over-correction and help stabilize run outcomes.
Before each run, confirm control layout and game settings.
bullets: Review sensitivity and preferred input mode
Start with simpler angles and shorter runs
Avoid changing multiple control-related settings at once

sections.heading: Session flow
paragraphs: A standard session starts from the game start flow, then moves into active play, level completion checks, and score-based progression loops.
Use a short session first if you are warming up, then increase play duration as control consistency improves.
bullets: Start a fresh run and focus on steady movement patterns
Track the immediate replay goal: finish one level cleanly before stretching time
Use power-ups only to support consistency, not to mask control drift

sections.heading: Game modes and session intent
paragraphs: ZXBrick focuses on a single-player progression loop for the documented modes.
Use mode choice to match your objective: clean practice rounds for consistency or longer sessions for score growth.
Keep mode intent stable for at least one run cluster so results remain comparable.

sections.heading: Levels
paragraphs: ZXBrick confirms 100 handcrafted levels in its marketing and app metadata.
The level loop should be treated as your primary replay structure for short and medium sessions.
Use a fixed target count for each session rather than jumping levels every run.

sections.heading: Power-ups
paragraphs: Power-ups are support systems that can alter risk windows and pace within level sessions.
They are available at the game-design level and are intended to reinforce replay habits, not replace basic movement control discipline.
Avoid using power-ups as a substitute for good movement discipline. Build one repeatable rhythm, then incorporate the available boosts deliberately.
bullets: Pick a single variable and keep it stable for one replay cycle
Use shorter sessions when testing a new power-up
Record one thing that improved or worsened performance

sections.heading: Achievements
paragraphs: The current public site data includes milestone goals tied to game volume and brick completion milestones.
Pick one achievement target per session.
Use short sessions to hit each target with focused goal consistency.
bullets: Play 10 Games
Play 25 Games
Play 50 Games
Play 100 Games
Destroy 500 Bricks
Destroy 1000 Bricks

sections.heading: BarkinMad Coins
paragraphs: BarkinMad Coins are the shared progression token model across supported ZX Series titles.
In ZXBrick, they connect gameplay participation with broader unlock opportunities.
Treat coins as a long-term loop, not a one-off bonus.

sections.heading: Leaderboards
paragraphs: ZXBrick currently publishes a highest-score leaderboard.
Use this list to compare replay consistency across sessions and validate training results.
Use one leaderboard goal per short cycle to prevent noisy comparison behavior.
bullets: Highest Score

sections.heading: Tips and strategy
paragraphs: Keep one control rhythm for your opening two sessions.
Increase complexity only after the first objective is repeatable across multiple runs.
Pick a small objective, complete three consistent runs, then reset the objective for the next cluster.

sections.heading: Accessibility and readability
paragraphs: This complete guide page exists to make ZXBrick content directly readable by screen readers, search crawlers, and accessibility-first users.
Each topic remains available through standard app documentation routes with images available where needed for human review.
Use this page as the canonical text source when sharing deep links for onboarding or documentation parity.

faq.question: Is ZXBrick part of the ZX Series?
faq.answer: Yes. ZXBrick is part of the [ZX Series](/zx-series).

faq.question: Does ZXBrick include BarkinMad Coins?
faq.answer: Yes. ZXBrick supports shared BarkinMad Coins through the ZX Series model.

faq.question: Does ZXBrick support leaderboards?
faq.answer: Yes. The current published category is the highest score leaderboard.

featureShowcases.summary: The Guide connects the full ZXBrick documentation set into one route through setup, play, progress, and support.
body: This matters because the ZX Series pages should feel consistent across all games. The guide helps players move from the overview into the exact page they need without losing context.
Players use this page as the main map when they want a complete explanation rather than a single topic.
bullets: Connects setup, controls, modes, tips, achievements, leaderboards, FAQ, and screenshots.
Keeps the documentation route consistent across ZXSnake, ZXBrick, ZXPong, and ZXSpace.
Uses existing artwork as the current guide placeholder.

featureShowcases.heading: From play to progress
featureShowcases.summary: ZXBrick documentation explains what players do during a session and what they can review afterward.
body: This matters because arcade games are easy to start but deeper when players understand scoring, progress, and goals.
Players use the guide to connect active play with achievements, leaderboards, BarkinMad Coins, and support pages.
bullets: Supports both first-session learning and repeat play.
Keeps progression pages tied to gameplay.
Makes image presentation consistent with StudioDash and Game of Darts pages.
